summary: DHH reflects on his inability to throw away old belongings, despite knowing that keeping them only delays their inevitable disposal. He catalogs items he's held onto far too long — jeans that haven't fit since 1999, duplicate gaming t-shirts, and yellowed whites — and admits that every inventory session proves the same lesson. He frames it as a personal rule he can never seem to follow: if you're in doubt, just toss it.
key_points: Keeping items you're unsure about only delays their inevitable disposal
Repeated personal experience proves the pattern, yet the lesson never sticks
The principle mirrors 'You Aren't Gonna Need It' (YAGNI) from software development
money_quotes: If you don't toss it now, it'll merely be a temporary stay on the inevitable execution
I end up refusing to acknowledge a simple, proven fact whenever I'm in doubt about getting rid of something
key_insight: When you're in doubt about whether to keep something, the answer is almost always to get rid of it now rather than delay the inevitable.
